title: Expanding Remote Monitoring Access Act
plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for people on Medicare to get paid remote health monitoring services. Right now, Medicare only pays for these services if patients collect health data for at least sixteen days each month. The bill would change this so Medicare would pay if patients collect data for just two days in a thirty day period. This change would last for two years while the government studies whether it works well. After one year, officials must write a report to Congress about how effective the new rule is and suggest better ways to pay for remote monitoring based on what patients need and how much it costs.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Expanding Remote Monitoring Access Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has found the following facts. Remote monitoring lets patients manage their health conditions from home while their doctors watch over them. This can make patients healthier, cost less money over time, and give patients more care choices. In two thousand nineteen, the Department of Veterans Affairs found good results from this approach. Veterans who used remote patient monitoring needed fifty three percent fewer days in VA hospital beds and thirty three percent fewer hospital admissions. Right now, Medicare requires doctors to collect sixteen days of patient data over a thirty day period before they can bill Medicare for remote monitoring services. This rule applies even when collecting data for that long is not needed to keep the patient healthy and safe. This limit reduces the use of remote monitoring in cases where it could help patients and save money for the health system. In the two thousand twenty one Physician Fee Schedule, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services made a temporary rule to lower the required time from sixteen days to two days within a thirty day period. But this only applied to people who had been diagnosed with COVID nineteen or were suspected of having it. This short term change showed the long term need to look again at the minimum time required for doctors to bill for remote monitoring. When making the two thousand twenty one Physician Fee Schedule, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services studied comments that supported permanently lowering the minimum required time for all patients, not just those with COVID nineteen. The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services agreed that a full sixteen days of monitoring may not always be reasonable and necessary. But they did not change the sixteen day per thirty day period minimum for all patients because they felt they had not received specific clinical examples to help them understand when fewer days of monitoring would be medically reasonable and necessary and allow a doctor to provide clinically meaningful care. Clinical evidence shows many cases where fewer than sixteen days of monitoring within a thirty day period provides clinically meaningful care. These include situations where sixteen days of monitoring per thirty day period may not be needed to show that a patient has sleep apnea. A patient taking narcotic pain medicine may need their breathing monitored only while taking the medication. A patient with a chronic condition like diabetes, heart failure, or obesity may have their weight monitored over a longer time, but it is not right for them to step on a scale sixteen or more times in each thirty day period. A patient whose blood pressure or oxygen levels are watched during physical therapy may not need sixteen days of monitoring in each thirty day period since physical therapy is often ordered twice weekly. A patient who wears a heart monitor to measure heart palpitations may wear the monitor all the time, but the data only needs to be collected when the person is having symptoms. A patient with high blood pressure is often monitored for long term management of this condition on more of a weekly basis, only needing more frequent data collection for active monitoring when medication or doses change. A patient who has Muscular Sclerosis or Muscular Dystrophy may benefit from a doctor tracking the patient's exercise between visits to monitor certain body functions like muscle movement but may not produce sixteen days of data in a thirty day period. A patient who needs a total joint replacement may simply need testing before surgery to establish starting points, including gait, force, activity, heart rate and other factors and then compare before surgery and after surgery function. For a patient with problems with urination, male urine flow data from the patient can be collected in two to four days in a row. Remote monitoring may let a doctor assess a patient's compliance, range of motion, and response to physical therapy and occupational therapy programs even though many such programs are less than sixteen days per month. Monitoring cognitive behavioral therapy for less than sixteen days in a thirty day period may provide clinically meaningful care while helping control a patient's anxiety and other symptoms. A patient with breathing problems may not need a full sixteen days of monitoring inhaler use to get clinical benefits from remote monitoring. A two day minimum time would allow Medicare coverage of the full range of remote monitoring services that can help a patient without preventing different payments for individual remote monitoring services based on patient severity and cost.

sections.title: Section 3 Ensuring Appropriate Access to Remote Monitoring Services Furnished Under the Medicare Program
sections.text: Despite any other law, the Secretary of Health and Human Services must make sure that remote monitoring services provided under Medicare are paid for with a minimum of two days of data collection over a thirty day period. This applies during the time period starting on the day this law is passed and ending two years after that date. This rule applies regardless of whether the person getting these services has been diagnosed with COVID nineteen or is suspected of having it. The Secretary must submit a report to Congress no later than one year after this law is passed. Before writing the report, the Secretary must talk with the groups listed in this section. The report must include a summary and analysis of past experience with remote monitoring services being paid for under Medicare with a minimum of two days of data collection over a thirty day period. The report must also include recommendations for creating a payment model that considers how sick patients are and the cost of providing remote monitoring services. This could include creating different payments for periods with different lengths of time, such as fewer than and more than sixteen days. The report must analyze and justify the right place of service and supervision requirements for non clinical staff who review and escalate patient data and provide recommendations. The report must also analyze the estimated savings from earlier treatments and fewer days of hospital stays among patients who get remote monitoring services. The groups the Secretary must talk with include relevant agencies within the Department of Health and Human Services, including the Inspector General for issues about waste, fraud, or abuse. The groups also include the Department of Veterans Affairs, including the Office of Connected Care. The list includes licensed and practicing osteopathic and allopathic physicians, anesthesiologists, physician assistants, and nurse practitioners. It includes hospitals, health systems, academic medical centers, and other medical facilities, such as acute care hospitals, cancer hospitals, psychiatric hospitals, hospital emergency departments, facilities providing urgent care services, ambulatory surgical centers, Federally qualified health centers, rural health clinics, and post acute care and long term care facilities. The groups include medical professional organizations and medical specialty organizations. They include organizations with expertise in developing or operating innovative remote monitoring services technologies. The list includes beneficiary advocacy organizations, the American Medical Association Current Procedural Terminology Editorial Panel, commercial payers, and any other group the Secretary thinks is appropriate. In this section, remote monitoring means remote physiologic monitoring and remote therapeutic monitoring. Remote physiologic monitoring means monitoring and analysis of body functions that is not done face to face and is used to understand a patient's health status. This includes collecting and analyzing patient body function data that are used to develop and manage a treatment plan related to chronic or acute conditions. Remote therapeutic monitoring means using medical devices to monitor a patient's health or response to treatment using data that is not about body functions.
